analyst_observations: The skill produces fundamentally different output type than baseline. Baseline agents default to code-validation mode (checking findings against actual repo), while the skill forces adversarial threat modeling mode with structured abuse cases.

Eval 3 baseline is the starkest contrast: without the skill, the agent declared all 5 findings false positives by validating against Aerie's actual code. The skill version correctly treated them as hypothetical and generated 9 abuse cases.
Eval 1 baseline also validated findings against code, downgrading 3 of 5 findings. The skill version accepted findings at face value AND validated code, producing richer compound analysis.

BIL score ordering is the main structural weakness: 2 of 3 with-skill runs had imperfect descending order. The compound risk case (AC-07) tends to be placed last as a 'summary' case rather than by BIL score.
Mode A (eval 2) included a Findings-to-Abuse Mapping table with 'No finding' entries. The skill template includes this section always but it adds noise in Mode A. Consider making it conditional.
Token usage is ~15% higher with the skill (47.9k vs 41.8k avg), with most of the delta in eval 1 where the skill does both threat modeling AND code validation.
Time is ~46% longer with the skill (176s vs 121s avg), reflecting the additional reasoning steps in PACE methodology.
